title: Conflict Resolution

jokes.text: No. 'Cause I'm in a band. We really rock. Yeah. I mean, it's inevitable.
jokes.setup: Asked about finding a band for a wedding
jokes.punchline: Confidently declares his own band will obviously be chosen despite likely being terrible
jokes.explanation: Classic Kevin overconfidence - perfectly in character for him to assume his terrible band would be chosen

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Oh, we're obviously going to get the gig. I mean, we're pretty good. We've been practicing in my mom's basement for like three weeks now.

jokes.text: She hears me arranging my social life. And we both have to hear Dwight order deer urine over the Internet.
jokes.setup: Complaining about overhearing wedding planning
jokes.punchline: Escalates to the absurd reality of Dwight ordering deer urine online
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail - only Dwight would order deer urine, and Jim's matter-of-fact delivery makes the absurdity funnier

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I can't believe I have to hear about wedding napkins all day.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's just a few details, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A few details? Yesterday you spent twenty minutes debating the thread count of tablecloths.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Amateurs. When I get married, there will be no such frivolity. I've already ordered deer urine online to mark the perimeter of the ceremony.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Deer urine? Dwight, why would you—

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: To establish dominance and ward off weaker males. It's what nature intended.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Right. Of course it is.

jokes.text: There are a few people I decided not to invite, and that might make things kind of awkward, but it's my wedding. And I don't want anyone there who has called me a hussy.
jokes.setup: Explaining why some coworkers aren't invited to her wedding
jokes.punchline: The specific and petty reason of being called a 'hussy' while delivering it sweetly
jokes.explanation: Pam's passive-aggressive approach and the old-fashioned word 'hussy' create gentle cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Well, I just think that some people at the office have been kind of mean to me, and I don't really want them there. Like, Ryan called me a hussy once, but he said it really sweetly, so I'm still not inviting him.

jokes.text: Fantastic Sams, Adult Cut Plus. Comes with a shampoo and blow-dry.
jokes.setup: Getting ready for ID photos
jokes.punchline: Proudly announcing his bargain haircut deal with unnecessary specificity
jokes.explanation: Pure Creed - treating a discount haircut like a luxury experience with complete sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I got this haircut for five bucks. And that included the shampoo.

jokes.text: On or off? Off. Okay. Oh, what is on your face? Is that a disguise?
jokes.setup: Taking ID photos, photographer asking about glasses
jokes.punchline: Photographer's confusion at Dwight's appearance, calling it a disguise
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy of Dwight looking so strange that a professional photographer thinks it's a costume

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Are you wearing glasses?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: So you're not wearing glasses.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Correct.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Then what is this?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is my face.

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Is this some kind of disguise?

jokes.text: Last year, I came to work with my spud gun in a duffel bag. I sat at my desk all day with a rifle that shoots potatoes at 60 pounds per square inch. Can you imagine if I was deranged?
jokes.setup: Complaining about office security
jokes.punchline: Casually admitting to bringing a weapon to work, ending with 'if I was deranged' implying he might be
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark - Dwight's matter-of-fact delivery about bringing weapons while questioning his own sanity is peak character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I bring a gun to work every day. It's in my desk drawer. I could pull it out at any time. But I don't, because I have discipline. Unlike some people, if I was deranged.

jokes.text: It's like child abuse! I say, if Jesus saw that, he'd freak out! He'd freak out, Toby!
jokes.setup: Complaining about Angela's baby poster
jokes.punchline: Escalating from child abuse to invoking Jesus's hypothetical horror
jokes.explanation: Oscar's dramatic overreaction to a cute poster, escalating to religious outrage, is perfectly overwrought

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Angela has this poster of a baby on her desk, and I'm like, 'That's not even your baby.' And she's like, 'It's inspirational.' No, it's creepy. It's like having a poster of someone else's child in your cubicle. That's child abuse.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: If Jesus saw that poster, he would weep. He would actually weep. Not because of the baby, but because Angela thinks that's inspirational.

jokes.text: I forget, are you guys dating? No.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to understand their conflict
jokes.punchline: Assuming their passionate argument means they're romantically involved
jokes.explanation: Michael's oblivious misreading of the situation - thinking workplace conflict equals romance

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. So you two are... together?

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: But you were just yelling at each other with such passion. I figured that meant you guys were, you know, in love or something.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Michael, we were arguing about accounting practices.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Right, right. The language of love.

jokes.text: My Shaolin temple style defeats your monkey style.
jokes.setup: Explaining five styles of conflict resolution
jokes.punchline: Immediately making kung fu references instead of understanding business concepts
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to take business seriously, immediately turning conflict styles into martial arts

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: There are five styles of conflict resolution: avoiding, accommodating, competing, compromising, and collaborating.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Avoiding is like a crane kick to the face. Accommodating is like... okay, imagine you're in the Karate Kid, and Mr. Miyagi is telling you to wax on, wax off.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Competing is like when the Cobra Kai strikes first, strikes hard, no mercy. Compromising is more of a Kung Fu Panda philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And collaborating is when you're doing that thing where two martial artists become one and they can read each other's minds.

jokes.text: Win-win is number four. And number five is win-win-win. The important difference here is with win-win-win, we all win. Me, too. I win for having successfully mediated a conflict at work.
jokes.setup: Explaining conflict resolution styles
jokes.punchline: Making up 'win-win-win' so he can personally benefit from mediating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - inventing a concept just to ensure he benefits personally from helping others

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not superstitious, but I am a little stitious. So I've developed a system. When two people are in conflict, I don't choose a side. I find a win-win solution.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Win-win-win. The third win is for me, because I'm the one who came up with it.

jokes.text: It makes me feel like the babies are the true artists, and God has a really cute sense of humor.
jokes.setup: Angela defending her baby poster using 'I' statements
jokes.punchline: Her pretentious art interpretation of what's clearly kitsch
jokes.explanation: Angela's pseudo-intellectual justification for terrible art, perfectly capturing her pretentious nature

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I feel like this is a very important piece of art. It speaks to the purity of childhood and the innocence we've all lost.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I interpret the composition as a meditation on vulnerability and our need to protect what's sacred in this world.

jokes.text: This is so much more offensive to me than hard-core porn.
jokes.setup: Oscar continuing his rant about the baby poster
jokes.punchline: Declaring cute babies more offensive than hardcore pornography
jokes.explanation: Hilariously extreme comparison that reveals Oscar's pretentiousness while being quotably absurd

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: You know what? Cute babies are more offensive than hardcore pornography. At least pornography is honest about what it is.

jokes.text: How about Angela makes the poster into a t-shirt, which Oscar wears. That way he can never see it and whenever she looks at Oscar, she can see it.
jokes.setup: Trying to find a creative solution to the poster conflict
jokes.punchline: Proposing an utterly ridiculous solution that technically works
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist solution that's technically logical but completely impractical - great structural comedy

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What if we just... hung the poster upside down? That way it's still there, but it's also not there.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, that doesn't solve anything.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: No, no, think about it. If it's upside down, technically it's a different poster. So we're not violating the rules.

jokes.text: How about Angela can keep it up on Tuesdays and Thursdays? Okay. That is called a compromise. And it is style three, and it is not ideal.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests sharing the poster on certain days
jokes.punchline: Michael dismissing a reasonable solution because it doesn't fit his preferred 'style'
jokes.explanation: Michael's rigid adherence to his conflict resolution 'system' over practical solutions

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, no. That's not how we do things here. We need to commit to a vision. A poster is a statement. It's a lifestyle choice.

jokes.text: That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Angela says 'You solved it?'
jokes.punchline: Michael's automatic sexual innuendo response
jokes.explanation: Standard 'that's what she said' - not particularly clever setup but essential to Michael's character

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: You solved it?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: That box is the special file in New York.
jokes.setup: Toby explaining he sends Dwight's complaints to a 'special file in New York'
jokes.punchline: Revealing the 'special file' is just a box in the office
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan reveal of Toby's passive-aggressive way of handling Dwight's constant complaints

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: I send all of Dwight's complaints to a special file in New York.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What kind of file?

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: A very special one.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: It's a box. Under my desk.

jokes.text: Creed is sick of looking at the redhead all day, and wants a seat facing the receptionist. Nice.
jokes.setup: Reading workplace complaints
jokes.punchline: Creed's creepy objectification of women, with Michael's approving 'Nice'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures both Creed's inappropriate behavior and Michael's inappropriate approval

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Women are like mermaids. If they exist, they're out of my league.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Nice.

jokes.text: Actually, I have a separate folder for complaints against Michael. This is January through March of this year.
jokes.setup: Michael bragging that no complaints are against him
jokes.punchline: Toby revealing a massive folder of complaints about Michael from just 3 months
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual gag subverting Michael's delusion - the sheer volume of complaints in just 3 months

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I have no complaints filed against me. None.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Actually, that's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: I have 47 complaints here from just the last three months.

jokes.text: You already did me. That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Discussion about which complaints to address
jokes.punchline: Another 'that's what she said' moment
jokes.explanation: Another standard 'that's what she said' - predictable but character-consistent

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We need to address the complaints we've received.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Some of these issues are really hard to get on top of.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: Wait. If someone has a problem with me, why would they withdraw it six months ago?
jokes.setup: Learning about redacted complaints about him from 6 months ago
jokes.punchline: Dwight's slow realization and confusion about the timing
jokes.explanation: Dwight's genuine confusion about why complaints would be withdrawn, missing the obvious implication

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Complaints? From six months ago? But I've been perfect for at least... three months.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Wait. Six months ago was... that was when I was still... no. No, that's impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Who filed these complaints? I demand to know who would dare question my performance before my recent transformation into an even better version of myself.

jokes.text: What the hell! Here is a Kelly complaint. 'Ryan never returns my calls.' Join the club.
jokes.setup: Reading Kelly's complaint about Ryan
jokes.punchline: Michael's bitter 'join the club' revealing Ryan ignores him too
jokes.explanation: Michael's moment of vulnerability - revealing Ryan doesn't respect him either

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Join the club. He ignores me too.

jokes.text: Dwight tried to kiss me. What? And I didn't tell anyone 'cause I'm not really sure how I feel about it.
jokes.setup: Pam revealing a secret about Angela
jokes.punchline: The shocking revelation that Dwight tried to kiss Pam
jokes.explanation: Major plot revelation delivered perfectly - Pam's confusion about her feelings makes it even funnier

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Okay, so there's something I need to tell you about Angela.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Dwight tried to kiss me.

jokes.text: Someone complained that the men's room is whites only.
jokes.setup: Reading more complaints
jokes.punchline: Racist complaint that reveals someone's horrible misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy that highlights workplace ignorance and prejudice

jokes.text: Then why is there a picture of a white man on the door.
jokes.setup: Stanley being told the bathroom isn't actually whites-only
jokes.punchline: His logical observation about the bathroom sign imagery
jokes.explanation: Stanley's perfectly logical response exposes the absurdity of bathroom signage - brilliant deadpan

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Well, if the bathroom isn't whites-only, then what's with all the white on the sign?

jokes.text: I don't like you.
jokes.setup: After resolving a parking dispute with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Jim's blunt, childish admission
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - Jim cuts through all pretense with brutally honest simplicity

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I parked in his spot on purpose. I wanted to see him mad.

jokes.text: I sprout mung beans on damp paper towel in my desk drawer. Very nutritious, but they smell like death.
jokes.setup: Someone complaining about Creed's old man smell
jokes.punchline: Creed's matter-of-fact explanation of his disgusting health food habit
jokes.explanation: Peak Creed - perfectly bizarre behavior delivered with complete sincerity. 'Smell like death' is perfectly economical

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: You know what? I eat a lot of fiber. My colon is very clean.

jokes.text: Solution: Angela, you are to make sexually suggestive remarks to Kevin that will make him uncomfortable. I accept your decision.
jokes.setup: Kevin accused of making Angela uncomfortable with sexual remarks
jokes.punchline: Michael's terrible 'solution' and Kevin's immediate acceptance
jokes.explanation: Michael's completely inappropriate HR solution and Kevin's enthusiastic acceptance is perfectly wrong

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Kevin, Angela says you've been making inappropriate comments. That's unacceptable. So here's what we're gonna do—you're gonna apologize to her.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And then you're gonna buy her lunch. Her choice of restaurant.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Sounds fair.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And finally, you're gonna compliment her appearance once a day for the next month.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Done.

jokes.text: I never smile, if I can help it. Showing one's teeth is a submission signal in primates. When someone smiles at me, all I see is a chimpanzee begging for its life.
jokes.setup: Photographer asking Dwight to smile
jokes.punchline: Dwight's bizarre pseudo-scientific explanation for not smiling
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Dwight logic - turning a simple photo into a dark anthropological theory. Highly quotable and character-perfect

dialogue.speaker: Photographer
dialogue.line: Come on, just smile for the camera.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Smiling is a sign of weakness. When you smile, you're essentially saying, 'I am not a threat.' Predators take advantage of that. I never smile. It's a sign that I'm always ready for confrontation.

jokes.text: This is humongous. I am not a security threat. And my middle name is Kurt, not fart.
jokes.setup: Jim looking at his ID badge
jokes.punchline: Revealing the badge lists his middle name as 'fart'
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag - juvenile but effective, and Jim's deadpan delivery sells it

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: My middle name is 'fart'?

jokes.text: Four years of malfeasance unreported. This cannot stand. Either he goes or I go.
jokes.setup: Dwight discovering his complaints about Jim
jokes.punchline: Dwight's dramatic ultimatum using the word 'malfeasance'
jokes.explanation: Dwight's over-the-top vocabulary ('malfeasance') for describing office pranks is perfectly pretentious

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim's insubordination and general buffoonery have reached a critical level. I'm calling a meeting with corporate to address his malfeasance.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Malfeasance? Dwight, I was just joking around.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This ends now, Jim. Either you shape up, or I'm filing a formal complaint that will result in your immediate termination. That's not a threat—that's a promise.

jokes.text: Cage matches? Yeah, they work. How could they not work? If they didn't work, everybody would still be in the cage.
jokes.setup: Michael suggesting cage matches as conflict resolution
jokes.punchline: His circular logic about cage match effectiveness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - completely flawed reasoning delivered with total confidence

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what solves problems? Cage matches. Two people go in, one person comes out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And that person has learned their lesson about conflict.

jokes.text: Yes. 5 bucks each and it was totally worth it.
jokes.setup: Complaint that everyone called Dwight 'Dwayne' all day
jokes.punchline: Jim admitting he paid people to do it and his satisfaction with the investment
jokes.explanation: Jim's casual admission of orchestrating elaborate pranks for small money - perfectly captures his dedication to annoying Dwight

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I may have paid a few people to do it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Best $40 I've ever spent.

jokes.text: This morning I found a bloody glove in my desk drawer, and Jim Halpert tried to convince me I committed murder. I think he may be the real murderer.
jokes.setup: Reading Dwight's complaint about Jim
jokes.punchline: The elaborate murder frame-up prank and Dwight's paranoid conclusion
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly escalating prank that shows Jim's creativity and Dwight's paranoia - the murder accusation is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, you submitted a formal complaint about Jim?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes, he's been framing me. Setting me up to look guilty of crimes I didn't commit.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What? What crimes?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Murder. He's been leaving evidence at crime scenes. My fingerprints, my hair.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, Jim hasn't left crime scenes anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's what he wants you to think. He's three steps ahead. He's always three steps ahead. I need protection.

jokes.text: When I went to save the child, I saw Meredith on the can.
jokes.setup: Jim told Dwight there was an abandoned baby in the women's room
jokes.punchline: Dwight's traumatic discovery of Meredith using the toilet
jokes.explanation: Perfect prank aftermath - Dwight's heroic intentions lead to horrifying discovery

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight went into the women's room and found Meredith on the toilet. He thought it was a baby.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It was the most horrifying experience of my life. I have filed a formal complaint.

jokes.text: That actually took a while. I had to put more and more nickels into his handset, till he got used to the weight. And then I just took them all out.
jokes.setup: Explaining how Dwight hit himself with the phone
jokes.punchline: The elaborate long-term conditioning prank with gradual weight increases
jokes.explanation: Brilliant long-term prank showing Jim's dedication and creativity - the psychological conditioning element is genius

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So what happened is, we've been gradually increasing the weight of the phone on Dwight's desk.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Like, a little bit heavier each day. He never noticed.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Until today when he went to pick it up like he always does, and he swung it up to his ear without thinking about it, and it was so heavy that it just... smacked him right in the face.

jokes.text: Yeah. I just moved it an inch every time he went to the bathroom. And that's how I spent my entire day, that day.
jokes.setup: Explaining how Dwight's desk moved closer to the copier
jokes.punchline: The meticulous nature of the prank and Jim's dedication to it
jokes.explanation: Another brilliant long-term prank - Jim's matter-of-fact admission that he spent his entire workday on this is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So we moved Dwight's desk closer to the copier. We did it really slowly, like a few inches a day, so he wouldn't notice.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And every time he'd leave his desk, we'd move it a little bit more.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: By the end of the week, his desk was basically in the supply closet by the copier.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: The best part? He never even realized it happened. He just kept coming in and wondering why the copier sounded like it was right next to him.

jokes.text: But the next day they feel great. I've never had one. They sound awful.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining shiatsu massage benefits
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's never had one and they sound terrible
jokes.explanation: Michael undermining his own metaphor by admitting ignorance and fear - typical self-defeating behavior

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Shiatsu massage is an ancient Japanese healing technique that aligns your chakras and releases tension from your body.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Actually, I've never had one. They sound terrible.

jokes.text: I have a girlfriend. Sure you do, Dwight. Sure.
jokes.setup: Jim suggesting they both transfer to Stamford
jokes.punchline: Jim's sarcastic dismissal of Dwight's girlfriend claim
jokes.explanation: Standard Jim dismissiveness - not brilliant but consistent with their dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What if we both transferred to Stamford?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I would never leave Scranton. Angela needs me here.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, Angela's really been missing you at the office.

jokes.text: One, two, three, smile. Try to smile. We resolved a lot today, everybody. Think happy thoughts.
jokes.setup: Taking a group photo after a day of aired grievances
jokes.punchline: Michael's forced optimism despite the obvious tension and unhappiness
jokes.explanation: Michael's oblivious optimism contrasting with the actual damage he's caused - solid cringe moment

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Everyone say 'cheese'! Come on, this is great! We all aired our grievances, we're closer than ever. Smile! This is the best day ever!

jokes.text: I'm the one who complained about you. I didn't know that Toby was going to write it down.
jokes.setup: Pam confronting Angela about the complaint
jokes.punchline: Phyllis revealing she was actually the complainer
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection reveal that adds awkwardness and shows Phyllis's passive-aggressive nature

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Angela, I know you filed a complaint about me. Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I didn't file any complaint about you.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Then who did?

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: That was me.

jokes.text: It was really hard getting a good picture of 15 people. He would not give me a discount, and eight tries added up.
jokes.setup: Trying to take group photos
jokes.punchline: Michael's admission that his cheap negotiating led to expensive multiple attempts
jokes.explanation: Michael's penny-wise, pound-foolish approach backfiring - consistent with his character flaws

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay everyone, gather around for the photo. Come on, tighter. Get in there. No, no, no, that's not right. Okay, everybody smile! Hold it... hold it... Perfect! Wait, someone blinked. Okay, take two. Alright, everyone back together. Come on, we're burning daylight here.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I negotiated a great deal with the photographer. Only fifty bucks for the whole session.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Turns out he charges by the photo. We're now at four hundred and seventy-five dollars.

jokes.text: But here's the thing about cage matches, sometimes you have to open the cage. And that is something that Toby will never understand.
jokes.setup: Michael's final philosophical thoughts
jokes.punchline: Extending his cage match metaphor while taking a final shot at Toby
jokes.explanation: Michael trying to sound wise while maintaining his petty grudge against Toby - typical ending

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, I have been in a lot of cages. I have fought many lions. But I have never, ever fought in a cage match against my actual nemesis. Until now. And his name is Toby.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Toby is the physical embodiment of everything that is wrong with the world. He represents the death of fun, the crushing of dreams, the slow erosion of the human spirit.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So I'm taking him down. Metaphorically. And literally, if HR allows it.

tmdb_overview: When Michael takes over conflict resolution duties from HR, chaos ensues at Dunder Mifflin.
insight: Cringe discomfort drives an 80-point episode with 42 jokes in 42 minutes.
